coredata.dc:description: Li-ion batteries are a key enabling technology for electric vehicles and determining their properties precisely is an essential step in improving utilization and performance. Batteries are highly complex electrochemical systems, with processes occurring in parallel on many time- and length-scales. Models describing these mechanisms require extensive parametrization efforts, conventionally using a combination of ex-situ characterization and systems identification. We present a methodology that algorithmically designs current input signals to optimize parameter identifiability from voltage measurements. Our approach uses global sensitivity analysis based on the generalized polynomial chaos expansion to map the entire parameter uncertainty space, relying on minimal prior knowledge of the system. Parameter specific optimal experiments are designed to maximize sensitivity and simultaneously minimize interactions and unwanted contributions by other parameters. Experiments are defined using only three design variables making our approach computationally efficient. The methodology is demonstrated using the Doyle-Fuller-Newman battery model for eight parameters of a 2.6 Ah 18,650 cell. Validation confirms that the proposed approach significantly improves model performance and parameter accuracy, while lowering experimental burden.

head.ce:title: Improving Li-ion battery parameter estimation by global optimal experiment design

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Li-ion batteries are a key enabling technology for electric vehicles and determining their properties precisely is an essential step in improving utilization and performance. Batteries are highly complex electrochemical systems, with processes occurring in parallel on many time- and length-scales. Models describing these mechanisms require extensive parametrization efforts, conventionally using a combination of ex-situ characterization and systems identification. We present a methodology that algorithmically designs current input signals to optimize parameter identifiability from voltage measurements. Our approach uses global sensitivity analysis based on the generalized polynomial chaos expansion to map the entire parameter uncertainty space, relying on minimal prior knowledge of the system. Parameter specific optimal experiments are designed to maximize sensitivity and simultaneously minimize interactions and unwanted contributions by other parameters. Experiments are defined using only three design variables making our approach computationally efficient. The methodology is demonstrated using the Doyle-Fuller-Newman battery model for eight parameters of a 2.6 Ah 18,650 cell. Validation confirms that the proposed approach significantly improves model performance and parameter accuracy, while lowering experimental burden.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries are a key technology in electrification of transport [3] and energy storage applications for a smart grid [1]. Continuous improvements of materials technology and cell design pose a challenge for engineers and researchers aiming to decipher aging mechanisms, design battery systems or control batteries precisely. Many different models have been developed for such purposes; they are commonly divided into data-driven, equivalent-circuit, or physics-based [2].

Data-driven models are purely empirical, and their predictive capability is based on trends in measured training data. Model predictions are accurate only within the training set and the model parameters do not have any physical meaning [2]. Equivalent-circuit models estimate and lump electrochemical processes with electric circuit elements [4]. They can accurately predict measured cell behavior, but similarly to data-driven models neither provide insight into internal states, nor offer physically meaningful parametrization. Mechanistic physics-based battery models are generally advantageous in applications with high demand for accuracy in cell voltage and interest in internal battery states.

Onboard an electric vehicle (EV), batteries are controlled by the battery management system (BMS) with tasks including state-of-charge (SOC) [3] or state-of-health (SOH) estimation [4]. With a BMS based on a physics-based model the electrochemical states in the cells can be monitored in real time [5]. Both the single particle model [8] and the Doyle-Fuller-Newman (DFN) model [6] have been proposed for BMS [7]. However, sufficient model accuracy and additional understanding of cell aging mechanisms can only be achieved with accurate parameter values.

These established physics-based models include many unknown parameters that require extensive experimental effort to determine [8,9]. Beyond battery control, physics-based models are used for the optimization of battery design [10], where accurate knowledge of model parameters is required to optimize mass loading or microporous electrode properties. Furthermore, they are used to study intercalation electrodes [11], where parametrization helps deciphering macroscopic behavior during complex insertion reactions or they are coupled with mechanical models [12] to estimate stress distributions on cell and pack level during battery operation. The wide availability and good performance of such models means that the challenge shifts from formulating an accurate model to that of precise and efficient parametrization.

The first step in parametrization is scouring the literature for published data. However, parameters are often unique to specific cell types, as they depend on choice of active material, geometry, and other factors such as formation protocol or shelf life. This implies that to best predict a real battery's behavior the cell at hand must be studied.

The next step would conventionally be characterization of the cell. A model is fitted to electrochemical measurements such as galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy [8], constant current cycling or dynamic drive cycles. These methods are based on electrochemical understanding of reaction limiting processes. Uddin et al. [13] use a 1C discharge to extract model parameters. Forman et al. [14] use a drive cycle for fitting. Reddy et al. [15] employ an experiment containing charge-discharge cycling and different magnitude pulses with intermediate relaxation. Approaches in the literature range from identification of the entire set of 88 parameters [14] for the DFN model to identification of parameter subsets [16] for the simplified single particle model [17] that does not consider electrolyte dynamics.

More thorough characterization is often based on half-cells (working electrode vs. a reference electrode or working electrode vs. a less limiting counter electrode) constructed from harvested electrodes, as half-cell models reduce the number of unknowns. Some geometric parameters such as electrode thicknesses or particle radii can readily be determined experimentally by destructive characterization of opened cells [9]. However, many parameters remain to be determined by fitting the model to the measured cell voltage. Crucially, model parameters can only be accurately determined in this way when they are identifiable, that is, when their impact on the cell voltage is sufficiently unique and larger than measurement noise. The experiments used for parameter fitting therefore determine the achievable parameter and model accuracy. If parameters are estimated from experiments that they are insensitive to, model performance will deteriorate in differing conditions.

The choice of data can intuitively be understood to effect parametrization. Rajabloo and Jokar et al. [18,19] illustrate specifically how parameter sensitivity depends on the experiment. Zhang et al. [20] studied best conditions for identification, and highlighted how parameters can be best identified where their sensitivity is highest. These sensitivities depend on battery internal states, such as the electrode state-of-lithiation (SOL). Optimal experiments, i.e., experiments with the highest possible sensitivity towards desired parameters, therefore exist.

Optimal experiment design (OED) aims to find an experiment that is optimal in terms of some statistical criterion and is a general-purpose tool [21]. Few publications study OED for physics-based battery models [22–25]. Forman et al. [25] perform a local sensitivity analysis (SA) for two parameters of the DFN model, the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) resistance and the electrolyte diffusion coefficient. They find optimal current trajectories where every current point is considered an OED variable. Park et al. [22] similarly use local SA to study the DFN model but include 18 model parameters. Pozzi et al. [23] use local SA and study seven single particle model parameters. They combine a series of constant current signals to form their optimal experiment, the current magnitudes are the OED variables. A recent extension on their work [24] outlines the advantages of a global SA (GSA) to the hitherto studied local SA method.

Two approaches have been proposed to define experiments for battery OED in literature. (1) Pre-determining sets of experiments with a combination of pulses, sinusoids, and drive cycles [22] and (2) designing an algorithm that can generate many different experiments based on several input variables. The search for the optimal experiment is then formulated as a second, outer optimization problem [25]. These OED variables could be current magnitudes and corresponding time intervals [23,24], or a discrete current signal [25]. Approach (1) is limited by the given designs and is also inefficient as it investigates many unsuitable experiments.

SA determines the impact of individual parameters for a given experiment [26]. Previous model-based experiment design studies by Pozzi et al. [23], Park et al. [22] and Forman et al. [25] use a local SA which investigates perturbations around a nominal parameter vector θ. The local sensitivity S
i is defined as the partial derivative of an observable y with regards to the parameter θ
i .

θ i

This expression is only constant and therefore independent of the nominal value θ i if the quantity of interest, for example the cell voltage, linearly depends on the parameter. As the DFN model is non-linear, a sensitivity calculated in this manner is only valid in the vicinity of the nominal parameter vector. In a conventional parametrization setting very little is known about the optimal parameter vector and an initial value cannot generally be assumed to be near the optimum. If the aim is to optimize experiments for parameter identifiability, computing sensitivities using Eq. (1) based on a nominal parameter vector will result in inaccurate sensitivities and non-optimality of the experiments.

Considering the discussion above, the present work extends on previous OED literature with three main contributions. Firstly, a GSA approach based on variance decomposition is employed. This global approach, in contrast to a local SA, allows exploration of the entire feasible parameter space and does not rely on an initial parameter guess that is near the optimum. Furthermore, GSA using Sobol's method [27] allows quantification of model parameter interactions, which is highly useful for parameter estimation.

Secondly, we propose a novel optimal experiment design criterion for GSA. This criterion is used to design a series of experiments each targeting a specific parameter. We thereby avoid sub-optimal experiments for one-experiment-fits-all scenarios where a single experiment is designed to be optimal for all investigated parameters and thereby cannot be optimal for each parameter individually. While this could increase required experimental time, the flexibility that short experiments provide in potential on-line applications must also be noted. This optimal design criterion could find experiments that completely mute contributions from all but the desired parameter.

Finally, we propose an algorithm that designs experiments only based on three OED variables, targeting equilibrium states, experiment dynamics and current magnitudes. This keeps the outer OED optimization problem dimension small while offering efficient exploration of the experimental space.

With these three contributions, we present a methodology suitable for the design of experiments that are optimally suited to excite selected specific model parameters. We first present the utilized DFN model and its parameters. Then, the GSA method is introduced, the algorithm to design optimal experiments presented, and the proposed global OED criterion derived. The optimization algorithm for the OED is briefly covered. The novel and unique combination of these components provides a directly link between experimental conditions and excitation of specific model mechanisms. We provide thorough experimental validation of the proposed approach on full and half-cell level. This confirms that global optimal experiment design enables fast-paced and non-destructive parametrization that significantly improves model performance and parameter accuracy over conventional parametrization and previously proposed OED methods for lithium-ion batteries.

The physics-based lithium-ion battery model used in this work to demonstrate the OED methodology is based on the work of Doyle, Fuller and Newman [6]. However, the proposed optimal parametrization strategy is not limited to this specific model but instead widely applicable for electrochemical battery models and beyond.

This model considers a one-dimensional battery slice containing negative and positive porous electrodes as well as the separator, all soaked in electrolyte. The negative and positive porous electrode consists of two phases. Firstly, a solid phase in which the active material can intercalate/de-intercalate lithium-ions during charge. The solid phase commonly also contains conductive additives and binder material. Secondly, a liquid electrolyte phase hosting and transporting lithium-ions within and between the electrodes. Transport in the electrolyte phase is described with concentrated solution theory [28] and considers mass transport through diffusion and migration. The two-phase porous electrode structure is described with porous electrode theory [29] which superpositions both phases. The particle domain describes lithium transport by means of Fickian diffusion in the active material. We assume an idealized spherical particle. The two domains are then coupled by electrode kinetics expressions to describe the charge-transfer between them. The parameters are either fitted, measured for the specific cell used in this study, assumed, or obtained from literature, as noted in Table 1 . The set of governing equations and their boundary and initial conditions describing a full cell are given in Table 2 . The equations are solved numerically for the potential in the liquid ϕ

e and in the particles c ±. In this paper we use PyBaMM [30].

For use in a half-cell model the counter electrode is instead modeled as a non-porous lithium electrode with Butler-Volmer kinetics (Eq. (18)). The working electrode (WE) is modeled as before. The reference electrode potential can then be defined as:

where the lithium metal reference electrode equilibrium potential is 0 V vs. Li/Li + and is assumed to be ideal, i.e., to not participate in any reaction.
We aim to determine a subset of 8 model parameters. The remainder (compare Table 1) are determined from a combination of literature data and physico-chemical characterization.

Prior to parameter estimation we perform an electrode balancing study using a C/20 rate full-cell discharge. This balancing step determines two stoichiometric points for both the positive and negative electrodes, relating half-cell SOL to full-cell SOC. Further details on this step can be found in [8,34,35].

We select model parameters from both electrodes that describe processes at different timescales and vary a lot in reported literature. These parameters have shown high sensitivity in previous studies or are expected to change with battery aging. The parameters are listed in Table 6. Literature shows that capacity related parameters have high impact on the model output [36]. As capacity determination is a trivial problem using full charge or discharge experiments, we omit these from the unknowns in this study. Diffusion in the active material can be rate-determining, making it the subject of many studies (e.g. [8,37]). Accurately determining the diffusion time constants in both electrodes is essential for model performance.

Due to particle cracking, the apparent particle radius in the positive electrode is expected to decrease during aging. Cracking could also impact the porous structure in the positive electrode and thereby the tortuosity, for which the Bruggeman coefficient is included. SEI layer growth will likely impact the porosity in the negative electrode, we therefore include both it and the SEI layer's thickness. Within the SEI layer itself only ohmic effects are considered.

Charge-transfer in both electrode reactions is modeled by the kinetic rate constant. All parameters are considered constant, meaning that SOL or other dependencies in diffusion coefficients and rate constants, particle size distributions or any further dependencies are not included. For some parameters (ε−, r +, Br +) the allowed sampling range has narrow constraints as reasonable values are easy to determine. For rate constants and diffusion coefficients large ranges are necessary as values at several orders of magnitude are commonly reported [8]. The SEI layer thickness constraints are based on the estimates by Safari et al. [32].

In this work, the exploration in the parameter space is based on Latin-Hypercube sampling. Contrary to Monte-Carlo sampling Latin-Hypercube sampling accounts for already sampled regions and thereby avoids clustering [27,38]. It measures the averaged contribution of the ith parameter to the total output variance in R

This approach is computationally expensive, a typical study uses (2 + N)n samples (i.e., model evaluations), where N is the number of unknown model parameters and n typically 500–1000 [26].

The efficiency can be increased by transforming the problem, for instance by Fourier transformation [40] or by establishing surrogate models. In this paper the generalized polynomial chaos expansion (PCE) [41,42] is used to construct a surrogate model. The PCE prescribes a polynomial functional to fill the space in between samples. This is generally accurate if models are smooth in their parameter space [42].

PCE is known to achieve spectral convergence for smooth model outputs [42], thus greatly decreasing the computational burden of GSA. The PCE is employed to approximate Y by linear combination of polynomial basis functions. The polynomial chaos representation of Y =

Sampling a parameter space can, even when selecting conservative constraints, yield certain samples that cause model crashes for some simulations. Consider an experiment at low SOC where some mild further discharge is applied, extremely slow diffusion in the negative electrode could result in zero surface concentration, a state in which the battery would long have reached a voltage shut-off potential. As all sample evaluations must return data of the same length for the GSA evaluation, this specific sample is unusable. We employ a nearest neighbor substitution [45] that replaces the crashed sample with the nearest feasible one. If too many samples crash reducing the allowed parameter sampling range should instead be considered [45].

Consider a space Ω ∈ R
n of all possible experiments that could be performed on a battery or rather, all possible current trajectories that could be applied to it. Here, n is the number of experiment design variables required to completely map this space. We propose an algorithm designed to explore the subspace
(43)

R m

Note that the number of experiment design variables m directly corresponds to the dimension of the outer OED optimization problem and therefore has large impact on the computational cost.

It is essential that the proposed algorithm is equipped to excite all model parameters that are relevant under the expected usage conditions. The transient nature of battery operation contains many different time constants [2]. Diffusive mass transport in the active materials, electrode kinetics, ion transport in the electrolyte, electron transport and double layer charging happen at different timescales [42]. To excite model parameters describing these mechanisms we include the experiment dynamics as an experiment design variable. A dynamic factor f
d of 0 corresponds to constant current whereas 1 corresponds to a highly dynamic experiment with current changes at every second sampling point. Literature has shown that identifiability and sensitivity to model parameters can be highly dependent on half-cell open circuit potential slope [18,46]. The SOC is therefore included as the experiment design variable f
soc determining the relaxed initial condition of the cell. Many model parameters have increased sensitivities for high rates [18,36]. This is accounted for with the rate-factor optimal experiment design variable f
r . These three parameters have practical implications and allow for convenient experiment constraints based on real world applications, such as allowed C-rates or a prescribed SOC window. The implemented algorithm is briefly described in Algorithm 1.

Additional design constants like the experiment sampling rate are set and remain constant. Furthermore, to make sure experiments remain within the 0–100 % SOC window, further discharge when initializing close to zero SOC or further charge when initializing at high SOC is not allowed. Experiments with trajectories entering these extreme SOCs are adjusted in the same manner. The experiment design variables and parameters are listed in Table 3 . Algorithm 1

We define an optimality criterion based on the work by Schenkendorf et al. [47], who present a coupled set of optimality criteria (reproduced in Table 4 ) for global OED based on Shannon entropy. S

is the Sobol index at a specific time. The first objective Eq. (44) balances the contributions by all model parameters, as simply maximizing the sum would lead to one parameter dominating. The second objective Eq. (45) minimizes concurrency of parameter sensitivity. The third objective Eq. (46) minimizes interdependence and the last objective Eq. (47) maximizes the total model variance. All these properties are desirable, however in a multi-objective problem the solution can only be Pareto-optimal and furthermore depends strongly on how the multiple objectives are weighted.

We therefore simplify the four criteria in Table 4 by investigating optimality only with regards to a single parameter per experiment. For a single parameter i Eq. (46) with S

Furthermore, Eq. (45), which aims to minimize concurrent sensitivity of several model parameters, can be disregarded if only a single parameters influence is concerned. Eq. (44) can similarly be discarded as it aims to balance sensitivities of all parameters. If only one parameter is targeted, the balance must instead aim to maximize this parameter's influence, as in Eq. (48). Recall that Sobol indices are defined as contributions to model variance and in the absence of higher order interactions sum up to 1. By maximizing contributions from θ i , all other contributors are therefore simultaneously minimized. In the same manner, parameter interdependence and concurrent sensitivity is minimized.

The optimality criterion Eq. (48) transforms the singular multi-objective problem in Table 4 into a set of consecutive single-objective problems. The variance criterion Eq. (47) is disregarded in this study as we believe it would unjustly inflate the importance of the rate-factor and require definition of weights for the multi-objective optimization problem.

Previous sections introduced necessary tools for a single iteration of the OED problem. The optimization problem in Eq. (48) is non-convex and the cost function contains an expensive GSA requiring on the order of 1000 model evaluations. Bayesian optimization is a method suitable for such cases of high cost and unhelpful problem structure [48]. The basic principle has three steps. In the first step prior information about the problem is used to construct an estimate of the cost function, commonly as a concatenation of Gaussians. This includes previous objective evaluations or knowledge of model structure. The second step suggests a new probe, i.e., the next point to evaluate the model at. This is based on the previously constructed prior. The third step uses the new information to construct a posterior, an updated belief. These three steps constitute one Bayesian optimization iteration. This method is highly reliant on the way new probes are selected. It is inefficient to simply test the surrogate-model's proposed maximum. Instead, a combination of high surrogate model prediction and large uncertainty is used to construct an acquisition function. This is a sub-routine that determines the optimal new point to probe. An in-depth review of the method can be found in [48]. In this work, the open-source python package provided in [49] is used. Due to computational constraints, we allow a maximum of 40 Bayesian optimization iterations. This corresponds to 40 sensitivity analyses for unique current trajectories generated according to Algorithm 1 for each model parameter. The completion of these 40 iterations required around 3 h of wall-time during parallel function evaluation on 16 cores of a Dell precision T7600 workstation.
After experiments are established and carried out, parameter optimization minimizes residuals between experiment and model.

We define a least-squares minimization problem as in Eq. (49) where θ
l and θ
u are the lower and upper constraint vectors equivalent to ranges given in Table 6. The objective function is the sum of squared residuals over every discrete measurement point. The problem is initialized at the nominal parameter values.

We employ the gradient-free algorithm in [50]. The algorithm first tests several exploratory steps and then initializes in the best-found starting point. While less efficient than gradient-based methods, it is a reasonable compromise as the stochastic initialization and solver restarts when slow process is detected help avoid local minima. We experimented with different algorithms including the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm used by Park et al. [22] and global algorithms such as differential evolution used by Uddin et al. [13]. We found that the former would often only find sub-optimal local minima while the latter converged slowly and required significantly larger computational resources. The employed gradient-free algorithm offers a compromise between computational time and accuracy.

Resulting parameter estimates ideally produce the best fit, i.e., the lowest error of any possible parameter set. However, as this optimization problem is not generally convex, local optima are possible and avoiding them a challenge [51].

An iterative parameter estimation scheme according to Algorithm 2 is employed. In the first iteration all unknown parameters
θ ̂
are estimated from the experiment belonging to the most sensitive parameter θ
i . Only θ
i is moved to the vector of estimated parameters θ ∗ and fixed for the following iterations. The second iteration, now with k
i − 1 unknowns, estimates the remaining parameters from the experiment belonging to the second most sensitive parameter. Again, only the remaining most sensitive parameter is removed. This continues until all parameters are determined. This algorithm iteratively refines estimates by reusing the previous steps' results as the initial guess for the next iteration. Some parameters optimal experiments (OEs) show significant sensitivity to more than one parameter. If this is the case, we then extend the estimation data from the OE to a second experiment with high sensitivity that we denote near optimal experiment (NOE). The threshold for inclusion of NOEs is set to 90 % of OE sensitivity. Algorithm 2

Sections 3.1 through 3.5 are components of the proposed optimal experiment design methodology. To elucidate their position and responsibility in the procedure, it is visualized in Fig. 1 . The gray circle contains the optimal experiment design loop, which is repeated for each investigated model parameter θ i (ref. Table 6). First, an initial experiment is constructed using Algorithm 1. This experiment is then analyzed using the introduced GSA procedure, yielding Sobol indices for all investigated model parameters for this specific experiment. Then, the optimality criterion (Eq. (48)) is evaluated to determine sensitivity of the target parameter θ i . This is used to update the Bayesian estimate of the cost function. From this assumed cost function, a new probe is suggested, leading to the next experiment to be analyzed by GSA. This iteration continues until a stopping criterion (e.g., 40 iterations) is reached, at which point the experiment that achieved the highest objective value becomes the designated optimal experiment for parameter θ i . When all optimal experiments are determined, they, together with the reference and validation experiments, are carried out on the investigated cylindrical cell. Once the experiments are carried out on the investigated 2.6 Ah cylindrical cells, parameter estimation by least-squares regression (Eq. (49)) is carried out for the reference parameter set and using Algorithm 2 for the optimal experiment design parameter set.

The cell investigated is a 2.6 Ah cylindrical cell manufactured by Samsung SDI. Electrochemical experiments on full-cell level were performed on a Neware BTS4000 battery tester. The OEs were performed in charging direction going from lowest to highest SOC. OE were started when the cell voltage drifted <1 mV per 100 s window at the specific optimal SOC. Three dynamic cycles are later used for validation purposes; their different properties are listed in Table 5 . The three cycles correspond to real world applications in stationary energy storage. They are service cycles for grid frequency regulation (set 1), peak shaving (set 2) and their combination (set 3). C/20, 1C and GITT experiments are performed to serve as the basis of a benchmark parametrization. The GITT experiment contains 234,150 s pulses between 3.4 and 4.1 V at a C/10 rate, each followed by 5 h of rest. All experiments are conducted in a 25 °C temperature chamber.

A fresh cell was opened to determine the cell area as well as electrode and current collector thickness. The laid out double-sided jelly roll measured 0.03835 m2 yielding a specific capacity of 3.389 mAh/cm2. Average particle sizes were determined with scanning electron microscopy. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy was performed to find the electrodes active material composition and calculate theoretical capacities and active material volume fractions. This showed that the positive electrode active material has a LiNi0.5Mn0.3Co0.2O2 composition (NMC532) whereas the negative electrode active material contains only graphite. Harvested double-sided electrodes were delaminated on one side with N-Methyl-2-Pyrrolidone and 15 mm diameter half-cells with a lithium metal counter electrode constructed. A Whatman glass fiber separator and a 1 M LiPF6 in EC/DEC electrolyte (LP40, BASF, 0 ratio) was used and its parameters taken from [33]. These half-cells were used to measure the voltage of both electrodes at C/50 rates during charge and discharge. The open circuit potentials to be used in the model was then derived as the average between charge and discharge. The half-cell capacity of the positive electrode and negative electrode was determined to be 6.48 and 6.75 mAh respectively.

The harvested material was also used to construct three-electrode cells with a lithium reference electrode to generate half-cell validation data. These experiments encompass pulses in charge and discharge direction at five rates from C/10 to 2C with equal charge throughput. Pulse-trains were performed at 20, 50, and 80 % SOC.

The reference electrode is a lithium ring fitted around a glass fiber separator manufactured by El-cell. The distance between reference and working electrode is set to 0.13 mm. Additionally required parameters for the half-cell model are noted in Table 1. Half-cell experiments were performed on a Biologic VMP3 potentiostat in a 25 °C temperature chamber.

The determined optimal experiment design variables are given in the supplementary material and the corresponding current trajectories for each OE are shown in Fig. 2 . Due to the way the dynamics factor is used in Algorithm 1, slight variations of experiment lengths are possible.

Several interesting observations can be made about these experiments. Both diffusion coefficients are seemingly most sensitive for smaller currents and low dynamics. Their sensitivities differ significantly, implying that diffusion coefficients in different electrodes could be separated from full cell data alone. The opposite applies for rate constants in positive and negative electrode k + and k −, both are best excited using dynamic high current experiments. In fact, both experiments' design variables and optimal sensitivities are very similar, this could be an indication that they are difficult to separate from full cell data. This clearly differentiates charge-transfer and active material mass transfer time constants (i.e., r + 2/D +). The radius is also used to calculate the volume specific surface area (Eq. (6)), i.e., the electrode area where charge-transfer takes place and therefore linked with the rate constant k +. The derived OE for r +, being connected with both diffusion and charge-transfer, can be viewed as a combination of OEs for the two effects it connects. The OE for the positive electrode diffusion coefficient, a constant current experiment, is already commonly used in the shape of a GITT pulse. However, for GITT, the relaxation after the pulse is studied as well.

While interpretation of the OEs can provide interesting parallels to already existing experimentation, they primarily provide a methodologically derived set of optimal parameter specific experiments for parametrization linking directly to the model. The average first order Sobol indices that are used to rank the experiments for the iterative parameter estimation procedure are shown in Fig. 3 . This figure also shows the normalized maximum variance. Sobol indices quantify a contribution to the total variance. If this contribution is smaller than measurement noise in the entire experiment, a parameter is practically unidentifiable.

The highest maximum variance of 5E−4 V2 in the OE for the positive electrode diffusion coefficient is very similar to that of the 1C discharge. For low variance experiments and small Sobol index experiments, i.e., w
sei , the standard deviation associated with variance
Var
is on the order of 1 mV. This is below the expected measurement accuracy and therefore implies that this parameter is not identifiable from the given data. The corresponding experiment is therefore only included as a NOE for the D + parameter and the parameter itself is not further considered in this beginning of life parametrization.

For some parameters highly specific experiments could be designed. Namely in the OE for D − the objective parameter is responsible for 90 % of the observable variance. This implies that little information about other unknowns is required for its accurate determination and estimating it in the first step is practical. On the opposite end of the sensitivity magnitude ε
− even in its OE, only contributes 3.5 % to the total variance.

The 1C experiment shows the highest sensitivity to the Bruggeman coefficient, as seen in Fig. 3. It is in fact higher than the corresponding OE, highlighting that OE are optimal only within the space of allowed experiments. However, the difference in sensitivity magnitude is minute and when considering experimental length, the OE still has a considerable advantage. The extension to NOEs introduced above means that for parameter estimation of k − and k + both respective experiments are used. For Br + we additionally use the D + experiment and for D + we additionally use the w
sei experiment (compare Fig. 3).

To evaluate the fitness of the PCE method 1000 Monte Carlo samples (i.e., different parameter vectors not included in the SA) are generated for each OE. The prediction quality of the PCE surrogate model for three random samples is shown in Fig. 4 . Further benchmarking of the PCE method against the conventional sampling-based GSA is included in the supplementary materials.

An electrode balancing was carried out for the investigated cell. The root mean square error (RMSE) for a C/20 discharge between 4.2 and 3.2 V was 2.7 mV after the optimization. The measurement and the fitted model prediction are given in Fig. 5 .

Using Algorithm 2 and the initial values listed in Table 6 , parameter estimation is performed to determine the seven remaining unknown model parameters from the OEs. As a reference, Park et al. [22] used several consecutive constant current cycles and a drive cycle. We compare the parameter estimation using OED with that on a combination of constant current (1C) discharge from 100 to 10 % SOC and 10 C/10 GITT pulses initialized between 90 and 10 % SOC. For this, both the GITT pulses and the 1C discharge are simulated and their concatenated residuals are used to construct the objective.

The resulting parameter values are given in Table 6. As the true values remain unknown, interpretation or analysis based solely on the estimates is difficult. We however note that the determined parameter values for OED or reference parameter set (RPS) can differ several orders of magnitude. This is most prominent in the rate constants and diffusion time constants, for both of which generous bounds have been set. The RPS could be advantageous for these parameters as their concentration dependency would naturally be averaged over the full SOC window.

Studying the errors for the estimation sets (eight OEs and the 1C discharge) given in Fig. 6 allows three observations. Firstly, all parameter estimates result from high quality fits. This refers to the eight blue bars for eight investigated OEs and to the green bar for the 1C discharge (the 10 GITT pulses are not included in the figure for readability). Secondly, it hints at how well obtained parameter sets can extrapolate from the fitting data. The OED parameter set performs only slightly worse than the RPS for the 1C discharge, whereas the RPS produces significantly higher errors for the eight optimal experiments. This is especially evident for rate constant experiments in positive and negative electrode. Lastly, considering that the OEs were designed to maximize sensitivity towards specific parameters, the associated error can hint at the parameter estimate accuracy. The high errors in kinetic rate constants simulations using the RPS for instance suggest that it will struggle with predictions for experiments with rate-determining kinetics.

Without knowledge of true parameter values, validation can be performed by evaluating predictive performance for a validation data set. This will highlight if OED parameter estimation could be used to improve model performance. However, the accuracy of a parameter set itself, i.e., its deviation from the true parameter values, cannot be sufficiently assessed in this manner as it must be considered biased due to the model structure [52]. The true values (if they exist) will likely not be optimal in terms of performance [8,52,53].

The validation using set (1) is shown in Fig. 7 a). The residuals plotted below show that the OED approach clearly improves the model prediction, reducing the RMSE from 15.5 to 4.3 mV and the maximum error from 37.5 to 14.3 mV. In Fig. 7 b) a pulse in charging and discharging direction between 20 and 80 % SOC (set (2)) is shown. The RMSE is reduced from 16.2 to 11.4 mV. For set (3) in Fig. 7 c), the RMSE for the RPS is 24.5 mV, while for the OED set it is 14.4 mV. The OED approach reduces model errors by 40–75 %, considering the RMSE metric and by 30–60 % considering maximum errors. These errors are summarized in Table 7 .

Set (1) shows the lowest overall errors and the highest relative performance gain between OED and RPS; this is likely due to the small SOC range and relatively low currents. The increased accuracy with the OED parameter set can be attributed to parameter estimates for kinetic rate constants that the reference experiments simply do not excite sufficiently. This can also be seen in the RPS' prediction of the OEs (compare Fig. 6), where it performs worst at predicting the OEs for the rate constants in positive and negative electrodes. It is therefore probable that these parameters were not identified accurately from the RPS. However, rate constants are known to have significant SOL dependence which is not considered in the model. The RPS might therefore contain estimates more accurate for the entire SOC range but less precise for narrow SOC windows, as set (1).

Set (3), which has the highest SOC range and largest currents has the highest overall errors. Model accuracy decreases with larger SOC range and applied currents for both OED and RPS. Errors for both parameter sets are higher at low SOC and high rate (Fig. 7 b). However, the anticipated advantage of the RPS for wider SOC ranges could not be observed. In fact, the RPS performs worse than the OED set in the entirety of the experiment.

Even though it performs worse than the OED set, the RPS parametrization matches the experiments well, with RMSE below 25 mV in all cases. One reasonable explanation lies in the importance of the electrode balancing step that has been used in both parameter sets. Another likely factor is the wide SOC coverage of the RPS that could make it advantageous for parameters with significant SOC dependence.

Full-cell model performance suggests that parameter estimates from OED are superior to those found by conventional means. However, as we estimate four parameters per electrode, we cannot rule out lumping one electrode's properties into parameters of the opposite electrode from full-cell data alone. To assess if the estimated parameters' values are in fact more accurate and separating properties of negative and positive electrode, we include validation on half-cell measurements.

Three pulse trains with rates between 0.1 and 2 C initialized at 80, 50 and 20 % SOC are performed. Half-cell potentials are measured against a lithium reference electrode. In the positive half-cell model, a contact-resistance term is added to compensate for the measured ohmic drop. This was not required in the negative half-cell model. The x-coordinate of the reference electrode is assumed to be at the center of the separator. This might shift in practice, as the used three-electrode setup compresses the separator slightly. We show the experimental result as well as the OED and RPS predictions and highlight C/5 and 2C pulses in Fig. 8 .

For the positive electrode in Fig. 8 a, c, and e the difference in performance between RPS and OED is slight. Both sets perform worst at low SOC (high half-cell lithiation degree) but match the experiment well at 50 and 80 % SOC respectively. The significant change in apparent resistance between 20, 50 and 80 % SOC shows that SOL-dependent parameter values are required to fully resolve the entire lithiation span. The four parameters investigated for the positive electrode have different estimates in RPS and OED. The diffusion time constant τ + is three times faster in the RPS. However, diffusion limitations are not observed in the measurement relaxation behavior and both time constants are sufficiently fast to assume they do not impact the results. The Bruggeman coefficient and the kinetic rate constant are therefore likely responsible for the slight deviation in model predictions. The impact of the Bruggeman coefficient implies the electrolyte diffusion time constant takes precedent with the thick separator used in the half-cell setup.

An assumed advantage of the RPS parameter set was that it would find parameter estimates better suited for the full SOC range. This could not be confirmed, as both parameter sets equally struggle with resolving the low SOC and high SOC experiments (Fig. 8 a, e). As in the full cell validation, errors increase at lower SOC for both parameter sets.

For the negative electrode results in Fig. 8 b, d, and f the OED parameter set has an advantage. It more accurately matches the apparent charge-transfer resistance. The kinetic rate constant k −, RPS must therefore cause an underestimation of the charge-transfer resistance. The difference of nearly one order of magnitude in the diffusion time constant τ− similarly to the positive electrode does not impact the relaxation behavior after the pulses, confirming that as for the positive half-cell the electrolyte is dominating when using a thick separator.

Both validation sets show that for accurate predictions at low and high SOC in the positive electrode the rate constant's SOL dependency must be considered in the model. This confirms results from full-cell validation where higher errors correlated with low SOC (Fig. 8 a) and implies they can be attributed to the insufficient SOL resolution of rate constant parameter k +.

Half-cell validation suggests that parameter values estimated using the OED approach are more accurate as they better match measured half-cell data. Both sets equally perform worse at low SOC. The largest improvement is seen in the negative electrode, where RPS kinetics are inaccurate. This half-cell validation is however found insufficient to validate determined diffusion parameters, as we observe electrolyte properties dominating due to the increased electrolyte diffusion length in the thicker separator. Neither half- nor full-cell validation can rule out bias due to model shortcomings and assumptions.

The presented global optimal experiment design (OED) method includes three main contributions to parametrization of electrochemical models:
•
Firstly, a global sensitivity approach is used and therefore no accurate initial guess for the parameter vector is required. This makes the proposed approach robust and applicable especially when parameter uncertainty is high, for instance in beginning of life parametrization. Additionally, it allows quantification of parameter interaction, which is useful in parameter estimation for physics-based battery models. Of the eight selected model parameters, seven were deemed sensitive and estimated using the designed experiments. Parameter estimates were several orders of magnitude away from nominal values, corroborating the need for global sensitivity for optimal experiment design.
•
Secondly, we directly use Sobol indices to determine experiment optimality. We propose a new design criterion for a sequential parameter estimation approach that simultaneously maximizes sensitivity towards a selected single parameter, minimizes contributions by other parameters and minimizes parameter interaction. In this way we derive experiments that are parameter specific, targeting e.g., diffusion coefficients in the active material and porous electrode Bruggeman coefficients. For several parameters, highly specific experiments (90 % specificity for the D − experiment) could be designed. A sequential parameter estimation procedure additionally has numerical advantages as estimates can be iteratively refined.
•
Finally, we develop an algorithm that explores the experimental space based on three experiment design variables. It allows setting practical constraints like maximum currents, state-of-charge (SOC) windows or experiment lengths that become relevant when considering real world uses. The employed reference parameter set (RPS) consisting of GITT pulses and a 1C discharge has a considerably higher experimental burden attached, as the GITT experiment alone required nearly 30 days. The designed optimal experiments showed that investigated electrochemical processes are sensitive in distinct and unique regions in the experimental space.

We demonstrated this methodology on a 2.6 Ah cylindrical cell using only eight model parameters, of which we disqualified the solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer thickness after the global sensitivity analysis (GSA) revealed it contributed only below the noise level. Due to computational constraints, higher order Sobol indices were neglected. Their inclusion would likely further improve the proposed methodology as the designed objective would then include minimization of higher order interaction.

Validation was performed both on full-cell and half-cell data. Full-cell validation showed that parameters estimated using the optimally designed experiments improved model performance significantly, lowering the root-mean-square error (RMSE) by at least 40 % over the three validation sets. Half-cell validation showed that model improvement stems from increased parameter accuracy on individual electrode level and highlights that kinetic parameters are not identified correctly in the RPS. The performance gain is more significant in the negative electrode. In the full-cell validation, an increase in error was observed at low SOC. The half-cell validation confirmed this behavior and additionally attributed it to the positive electrode rate constant.

Optimally designed experiments lower prediction errors while reducing experimental effort. The low errors achieved with the parameter set obtained even from the RPS indicate that a pre-screening approach is effective and that it is not necessary to estimate all model parameters to achieve good model performance. Next to the remaining seven model parameters identified through the optimal experiment design procedure, the four identified cell-balancing parameters are essential for model performance.

The algorithm designing experiments does not cover the entire space of possible experiments and only 40 iterations were allowed. Thereby, only 40 different experiments were investigated per model parameter. The determined OE is therefore likely only optimal within this set.

Future work includes further demonstration of parameter accuracy. Furthermore, the optimal experiments will be used to re-parameterize the aging cells to evaluate if aging modes correlate with parameter changes and if these changes can be detected through intermittent re-parametrization.
